msg: Explain the CAP theorem and give a real-world example for each tradeoff
stdout:
{
  "runId": "e23beeae-0c16-4406-9849-c2f0358c7251",
  "status": "ok",
  "summary": "completed",
  "result": {
    "payloads": [
      {
        "text": "**CAP Theorem** — in a distributed system you can guarantee at most **two** of three properties simultaneously:\n\n| Property | Meaning |\n|---|---|\n| **C**onsistency | Every read returns the latest write |\n| **A**vailability | Every request gets a non-error response |\n| **P**artition tolerance | System works despite network splits |\n\nSince network partitions *will* happen, the real choice is **CP vs AP** (you always need P).\n\n---\n\n**CP — Consistency + Partition tolerance** (sacrifice availability)\n\n*Example:* **ZooKeeper / etcd** — during a partition, minority nodes refuse reads/writes to avoid stale data. A distributed lock service *must* be consistent; serving a stale lock means two writers corrupt data. Unavailability is safer than inconsistency.\n\n**AP — Availability + Partition tolerance** (sacrifice consistency)\n\n*Example:* **Cassandra / DynamoDB** — during a partition, every node keeps serving requests; replicas may temporarily diverge (eventual consistency). For a shopping cart or DNS, returning slightly stale data beats returning nothing.\n\n**CA — Consistency + Availability** (sacrifice partition tolerance)\n\n*Example:* **A single-node PostgreSQL** — fully consistent and always available, but the moment you add a second node across a network, partitions become possible and you're forced to pick CP or AP. CA only exists when there's no network boundary (i.e., it's not really \"distributed\").\n\n---\n\n**TL;DR:** Partitions are inevitable in distributed systems, so the practical tradeoff is always CP (block until consistent) vs AP (serve now, reconcile later). Pick based on what's worse for your domain: stale data or downtime.",
        "mediaUrl": null
      }
    ],
